text: Television as a medium has helped us by showing us how women in big cities live and behave
comments: I coded this under gender equality because the quote refers to seeing other women living more freely and differently, which shapes girls’ understanding of what women can do.

text: It has given us the courage to express ourselves now
comments: This is about girls’ agency and voice. I coded it as nuanced decision-making because it suggests some emerging ability to speak, though not full decision power.

text: Our fathers and grandfathers may not listen to us but our mothers do and sometimes they fight for us too
comments: This shows girls are not fully heard by men, but mothers sometimes advocate for them. I coded it as nuanced restricted agency.

text: Since we go to school we have some exposure; we know that we can also become teachers if we want
comments: This is a direct occupational aspiration for teaching.

text: But we have to be careful since men are still the heads in the community and they are the decision makers
comments: This directly confirms that men hold authority and decision power.

text: Now we want to at least try to express ourselves
comments: This shows some emerging agency, but only partial, so I coded it as nuanced.

text: Yes, we must study
comments: This is a direct positive aspiration toward education, but not tied to one specific job.

text: If we don’t study then we will also spend our lives cooking and cleaning and brooding
comments: This contrasts the desired future with a restricted domestic life, indicating a strong aspiration-attainment gap if education is absent.

text: We need to study to be self-reliant
comments: This is a clear broad aspiration for independence through education.

text: If we want to lead better lives then we need to study well
comments: This is a general life aspiration linked to schooling.

text: Both boys and girls study up to class 12
comments: This suggests similar attainment for boys and girls at this level, so I coded no clear gap.

text: We should complete our graduation (Bachelors)
comments: This is a direct educational aspiration for college completion.

text: If we leave our studies mid-way then we will end up doing farming and rolling bidis
comments: This shows an unwanted fallback livelihood and therefore a strong gap between aspirations and likely outcomes.

text: We must complete our studies to get a decent job like a teacher’s
comments: This links study directly to a specific respectable occupation.

text: Yes, parents are as committed to their daughter’s education as to their son’s
comments: Direct support for equal parental commitment to girls’ and boys’ education.

text: Yes, there is no gender partiality here
comments: Clear perception of gender equality in schooling.

text: Our parents want us to study
comments: Supports the idea that daughters’ education is valued.

text: Most of the girls here in our village go to school
comments: This suggests girls’ education participation is common, so no clear gap by itself.

text: There are some who help in sowing and harvesting
comments: Girls participate in some agricultural work, but not all, so I coded this as nuanced division of labor.

text: We want to learn to operate the machines that boys do but we are not allowed
comments: This directly shows women are denied access to knowledge and practice around machines.

text: We want to learn to operate the machines that boys do but we are not allowed
comments: This also implies machines are considered boys’ domain, confirming the norm that men are better suited to mechanized farming.

text: Some of us carry the bundles of crop on our head
comments: This points to gendered manual labor expectations for girls.

text: But we also study; we also help our mothers at home
comments: This confirms girls’ domestic role alongside school and agricultural labor.

text: When we fail our exams
comments: Exam failure is identified as a direct reason education ends.

text: Due to poverty
comments: Poverty is a major barrier to educational continuation.

text: Some boys quit studies and migrate for work
comments: This indicates how livelihood pressures interrupt schooling.

text: Sometimes some parents ask their children to quit studies and help in earning a livelihood
comments: Family livelihood needs force educational discontinuation.

text: Sometimes girls get married and are forced to drop their studies
comments: Early marriage is a strong barrier to girls’ attainment.

text: Safety concerns for girls
comments: Safety concerns are a direct mobility-related barrier affecting girls’ education.

text: Due to early marriage
comments: Repeated as a top reason for ending education, indicating a wide gap.

text: Safety concerns: there is no school or college here in our village and we go to XX to study. It is about 3-4 kilometers and not safe for us
comments: This is a very clear mobility restriction affecting schooling access.

text: Therefore some parents feel hesitant about sending us to school
comments: This shows how safety concerns translate into interrupted education.

text: Early marriage: sometimes when girls get married early then they are forced to quit
comments: This explicitly links early marriage to girls dropping out.

text: We think about our future and want to do well and live well
comments: This is a broad aspiration for a better future.

text: But paucity of funds generally kills our dreams
comments: This is a direct statement of aspiration being blocked by money.

text: Sometimes the cultural societal norms also kills our dreams
comments: This shows social norms are also a strong cause of aspiration-attainment gaps.

text: But we hope to study well and settle down well
comments: A broad educational and life aspiration.

text: I want to be a teacher
comments: Direct occupational aspiration.

text: Many boys have migrated for better work options
comments: This indicates lack of local options and a gap between work aspirations and local reality.

text: Some also do agricultural activities
comments: Traditional agriculture remains a common livelihood path.

text: We should have patience and look for a job
comments: Clear aspiration for employment after studies.

text: We should try to get a decent job
comments: Direct aspiration for formal respectable work.

text: We should watch television and learn
comments: This points to learning and self-improvement after study.

text: We should learn cooking
comments: This reflects a gendered domestic expectation for girls.

text: We should not sit idle
comments: Broad aspiration toward productive activity rather than inactivity.

text: Women don’t use machines
comments: This clearly indicates women’s exclusion from technological knowledge and use.

text: Women don’t use machines because they think they are incapable of doing so
comments: This directly confirms the norm that men are more capable in mechanized farming.

text: Men prefer machines because it saves them time and physical stress
comments: This reinforces the association of machinery with men’s work and capability.

text: Yes, both young men and women have equal opportunities to learn
comments: This directly opposes the idea that women lack opportunities to learn.

text: Women have the opportunities but they don’t want to make an effort to learn
comments: This nuances the norm: opportunities exist, but actual uptake is seen as lower.

text: I don’t agree. I want to learn to use the machines that boys do but my father doesn’t permit
comments: This is a very strong statement that women’s access to knowledge is actively restricted by family authority.

text: Men move around a lot and they go to XX and other places and they learn. Even boys go. Our movement is restricted between home and school and so we are constrained
comments: This directly confirms women’s mobility restriction and links it to lower information access.

text: This is a very small village and there are hardly any opportunities for growth here
comments: This indicates strong structural limits on future aspirations.

text: Science has developed a lot today and there is a machine for everything
comments: Positive view of technological change and information spread.

text: Since machine is making the lives of men easier therefore they also like using them
comments: This reinforces men’s association with machine use.

text: We don’t have any machine in our village. The men get them on rent from XX
comments: Shows technology access exists but is controlled and used by men.

text: Women are scared to use machines
comments: Confirms women are perceived as less suited to machine use.

text: Farming does not pay well. So men use machines and spend the time they save by working elsewhere and earning additional income
comments: This reflects limited profitability in agriculture and need for additional income.

text: Yes. Men use machines like tractor to get their plot ready for sowing which women do
comments: Confirms men are associated with machine-based field preparation.

text: Men prefer to do those activities which can be performed by machines while women still like to do it manually
comments: This clearly confirms a gendered division of labor.

text: But most women are rolling bidis here and harvesting in the harvesting season
comments: This indicates women’s typical roles are bidi work and harvest labor.

text: They may not find it easy since though they also move around a lot but they are not involved in selling and so may not be aware of market prices and bargaining etc
comments: This directly states lack of market knowledge as a barrier for women.

text: They will face transport problems
comments: Practical transport barriers restrict independent production/marketing.

text: If they want to sell in XX then they may not find it easy because of transport problems
comments: This is a mobility-related marketing constraint.

text: Girls will find selling a major handicap since they are not exposed and informed
comments: This directly confirms girls’ lower information access around selling.

text: We hardly go anywhere
comments: Strong evidence of restricted mobility.

text: We will also face the same problems as the boys
comments: This suggests girls may face general market difficulties too, but the statement is less specific, so I used a lower-confidence smaller-gap interpretation.

text: No one actually decides who will do what work on our plot
comments: This opposes a rigid division of labor by saying work is not allocated in a strict gendered way.

text: The whole family works together and people do what they are comfortable in doing
comments: Strong indication of flexible and shared labor.

text: Usually family members do whatever they prefer to do and no one fights on this issue
comments: This also opposes a strict fixed division of roles.

text: There is no division of work
comments: Direct opposition to a gendered labor division.

text: Men and women don’t have separate plots
comments: Opposes a gendered split in plot ownership/use.

text: Women help their husbands on their plot
comments: This still reflects some gender asymmetry, so I coded it as nuanced.

text: Most work is done by men and some by women
comments: This suggests labor is shared but still weighted toward men.

text: Families work together on the family plot
comments: Shared family labor rather than gender-specific plots.

text: In joint families all members work together
comments: Strong indication of collective rather than divided labor.

text: Yes, she will have to ask her husband
comments: Direct confirmation that women need husband’s approval to plant a new crop.

text: Here women generally have to consult their husband on everything
comments: Very strong evidence of restricted decision-making power.

text: She will also have to ask other elder family members like her in laws
comments: Decision-making is constrained by wider family hierarchy too.

text: Man will his friends and his father
comments: Men have broader advisory networks.

text: He doesn’t need to ask his wife
comments: Directly confirms asymmetry in consultation and power.

text: He may consult her but it is not important
comments: Wives’ views are not central, so this nuances but still reflects lower female influence.

text: He will not let her join
comments: Direct restriction on women joining a local group.

text: Most women here work from home and he will want her to stay at home and work
comments: Confirms gendered expectation that women belong in home-based work.

text: No, he will not like the idea of her associating with any organization
comments: This suggests social suspicion around women joining outside groups.

text: He will not let her go for fear that she may end up making losses
comments: Restricts women’s movement to market and enterprise activity.

text: He will not trust her to get money for her produce and therefore he will sell himself
comments: This frames women as less capable in selling and handling market transactions.

text: Men always think they are more capable
comments: This is a broad statement of male superiority that fits the men-as-more-capable norm.

text: Depends upon how her business is doing. If she is doing well then he will let her concentrate on her work
comments: This suggests some accommodation of women’s own economic activity, so nuanced division of labor.

text: Yes, he will not insist on her help with his work
comments: Indicates some shift away from automatic expectation of women’s labor on husband’s work.

text: People will not talk well of her
comments: Direct evidence of negative social reputation for market-active women.

text: They will say she is hobnobbing with men and enjoying
comments: This is a clear sexualized stigma against women who move around in public markets.

text: But she is working for her family and hence no one should have any problem
comments: This is a defense of women’s public work and supports gender role flexibility.

text: Yes, she is doing it to support her family and so people should respect her for that
comments: This positively values a woman’s economic contribution.

text: Mark will have a tough time and will have to face the wrath of the community
comments: Strong evidence of stigma against men doing housework/support roles.

text: People will question him and the elders in his family will try to reason with him
comments: Community and elders enforce conventional masculinity.

text: His friends will stop talking to him
comments: Shows social exclusion for men who violate gender norms.

text: Mark will be happy at his wife’s success
comments: Positive reaction to wife’s enterprise success supports gender equality.

text: He will be happy he let her work
comments: This supports women’s working role, though still framed through male permission.

text: He will support her and encourage her to do better
comments: Clear supportive response to female economic success.

text: Gender equality is a great concept
comments: Directly favorable perception of gender equality.

text: Men and women should be treated equally
comments: Direct statement of equality.

text: We watch on television how women are also working in big companies and drive around in cars
comments: This shows awareness and approval of women in modern public roles.

text: Earlier girls were not allowed to work whereas today we have female teachers at our school
comments: This reflects positive change in women’s opportunities.

text: In our civics subject we are taught that we all have equal rights and opportunities
comments: Direct support for gender equality.

text: We should also have the freedom to study, to play, to eat and live as we want
comments: Strong equality claim about everyday freedoms.

text: Given a chance we can also prove that we are as good as the boys in every respect
comments: Direct challenge to male superiority and strong support for equality.

text: We are not weak by any means
comments: Directly opposes stereotypes of women as weak or inferior.

text: They will decide together and David will mostly let Diana decide
comments: This strongly supports women’s participation and influence in household decisions.

text: They are sharing responsibilities and managing their house together and so they will discuss between themselves before taking any decision
comments: This clearly opposes restrictive female decision-making by depicting a collaborative couple.

text: If David is busy then Diana will go
comments: This suggests women may sell produce under some conditions, so I coded labor division as nuanced.

text: David is more likely to go
comments: Men are still more likely to handle market selling, confirming gendered labor roles.

text: Yes they will implement the change
comments: This supports acceptance of new farming ideas that came through a woman’s network.

text: It is not bad to take risks and experiment
comments: Positive statement about innovation and experimentation.

text: There doesn’t seem to be much risk in this since her sister has already tried it out successfully
comments: This validates women’s innovation knowledge through successful example.

text: But he may say that her sister’s success doesn’t necessarily imply that they will succeed too
comments: This shows possible dismissal of women-linked knowledge, so I coded it as constrained information influence.

text: If the woman is wise and intelligent then she may just be able to persuade him to buy the machine
comments: This implies she does not have full independent control over inherited money and must persuade her husband.

text: Women will fight tooth and nail since it is her father’s money
comments: This explicitly asserts a woman’s right over inherited money, opposing asset restriction.

text: But it is not easy for a woman to defy her husband
comments: This confirms restricted female agency in practice.

text: It will depend upon their mutual understanding as also their financial condition
comments: This nuances the restriction by allowing some variation based on couple relations and resources.

text: But whatever be it men always do what they want
keyword: men always do what they want
comments: Strong confirmation of male decision dominance.

text: It will be a cake walk for him; first he is a man and second the money is his father’s; he doesn’t need anybody’s permission
comments: This directly confirms men’s much easier control over inherited assets.

text: He will buy motorcycle irrespective of whether his wife agrees or not
comments: This confirms men’s unilateral decision-making power.

text: It is always easy for men since they take most decisions anyway
comments: Direct confirmation of general male authority.

text: No young women cannot move around freely
comments: Direct statement of restricted mobility.

text: We cannot go alone to the market
comments: Direct statement of restricted movement to public market spaces.

text: Our safety is always a big concern
comments: Safety is explicitly identified as a mobility barrier.

text: Tongues start wagging if a young girl is seen roaming around alone
comments: This is direct social stigma around girls moving alone.

text: Yes, people think she is looking for some fun
comments: This is a sexualized stigma used to police girls’ movement.

text: Even married women don’t roam around freely
comments: Shows mobility restriction persists after marriage.

text: Her in laws and husband will not allow her to roam around alone
comments: Mobility is controlled by husband and in-laws.

text: Young married women face the same restrictions as us
comments: Indicates marriage does not remove restrictions for many women.

text: No that’s not true; young married women roam around freely with their husbands who take care of them
comments: This nuances the mobility norm: married women may move, but with husbands.

text: But not all young married women go out alone
comments: Again a nuanced statement: some freedom exists, but not universally.

text: Young married women have more freedom than us
comments: This indicates relative improvement after marriage, but not full freedom.

text: Very hard
comments: The group’s overall assessment is that it is very hard for women to sell in the local market.

text: She cannot go alone
comments: Direct restriction on women’s movement to market.

text: She might get robbed
comments: Fear of crime contributes to women’s constrained public mobility.

text: She will not know how to bargain and will generally find it tough since marketplace is dominated by men
comments: This directly frames women as lacking market knowledge.

text: She may not be able to deal with so many new people in the market
comments: This suggests discomfort with public mixed-gender market space; somewhat indirect, so nuanced.

text: We have already said that it is not easy for us to move around freely for safety reasons
comments: Direct summary statement of restricted mobility.

text: This is a small village and young boys go out frequently for work and have learnt wayward ways and so our parents are always very wary for us
comments: Parents’ concern about boys’ behavior is used to justify limiting girls’ movement.

text: Sometimes we go with our mothers and aunts or brothers
comments: This is a nuanced mobility norm: movement is possible with accompaniment.

text: What is the point in going if boys will embarrass us by passing lewd comments at us
comments: Strong statement of harassment as a stigma-based mobility barrier.

text: It is very difficult for a young woman to think of migrating before marriage
comments: Direct statement that pre-marital migration is highly restricted.

text: Our parents will not allow us to go anywhere till we get married
comments: Strong family control over girls’ movement and work migration.

text: We can go with our husbands post marriage
comments: Mobility is allowed only conditionally after marriage and with husbands.

text: No girl has migrated ever from our village
comments: This indicates an extreme mobility and opportunity constraint for girls.

text: No young girl has ever migrated
comments: This is descriptive rather than evaluative; it confirms no migration rather than a separate aspiration statement.

text: Girls get married at 16 and boys at 18
comments: Different ages at marriage reflect unequal expectations by gender.

text: Parents take marriage decisions
comments: Clear statement of restricted youth agency in marriage.

text: Love marriages are taboo
comments: Direct stigma against marriages based on personal choice.

text: We believe in monogamy; we don’t want to marry 10 times
comments: This expresses preference for one stable marriage.

text: We want one single happy marriage
comments: Broad life aspiration regarding marriage and family.

text: Our parents decide; they ask relatives and spread the word around that they are looking for a match for their son/daughter
comments: Marriage partner selection is clearly controlled by parents and kin networks.

text: Parents choose our life partners
comments: Direct statement of low agency in marriage choice.

text: No sooner does a girl turn 15 than her parents start worrying about her marriage
comments: Shows how girls’ marriage is parent-driven from an early age.

text: We cannot defy our traditions but we would like to be consulted
comments: This is a nuanced statement: girls accept tradition but want a voice in marriage decisions.

text: We want decent teetotaler guys who will shower us with his love and care
comments: This is a marital aspiration regarding the kind of husband desired.

text: Both the prospective partners must agree to the alliance
comments: Strongly supports mutual consent in marriage.

text: Girls should also have the right to accept/reject her marriage proposal
comments: Direct support for girls’ equal right to decide on marriage.

text: We should be consulted about our marriage
comments: This asks for a voice but does not reject family involvement entirely, so nuanced.

text: At 18
comments: This states the common age of first child and reflects early motherhood as a norm.

text: No. sometimes at that tender age complications arise and then they have to undergo an operation; it is very risky
comments: This clearly rejects very early motherhood as undesirable.

text: She should be mature enough to be able to birth and nurture a new life
comments: Supports later motherhood based on maturity and capability.

text: 22 is the right age
comments: This is a clear preferred age statement for first child.

text: 25 is the right age
comments: Another clear preferred later age.

text: 24 should be okay I guess
comments: Same theme of preferring later childbirth, but phrased less firmly.

text: No, no women don’t have any say in such matters
comments: Very direct confirmation that women lack reproductive decision-making power.

text: There is no planning for the first child; it just happens
comments: This suggests women do not control or plan timing of the first child.

text: Though she cannot decide when she wants her first child but she has a say in how many she wants
comments: This nuances reproductive agency: women lack timing control but have some say in total children.

text: We want that all of us should study
comments: Broad aspiration for universal education in the village.

text: No one from our village goes to college; we all want to complete our college education
comments: Clear aspiration for higher education.

text: How can anyone go to college when there is no college here?
comments: Direct structural barrier preventing attainment of the college aspiration.

text: Girls should not lose hope
comments: This is an aspirational encouragement statement.

text: We want good transport facilities and better infrastructure; we want a college and some training centers here
comments: This is a strong aspiration for infrastructure that would support education and skill-building.

text: We want jobs here not just for us but for everybody including our parents
comments: Broad aspiration for local employment and better livelihood conditions.
